text_blocks.snippet: Buying is best if you want to eventually own the car and drive unlimited miles. While monthly payments are higher, it’s the most cost-effective long-term option once the loan is paid off. Leasing is ideal if you want lower monthly payments, lower upfront costs, and the ability to drive a new car with a warranty every few years.
Review the key differences below to determine which option aligns with your driving habits and budget:

list.snippet: Costs: Higher monthly loan payments (average around $770) and typically requires a 10% to 20% down payment.
Ownership: Once the loan is fully paid off, you own the vehicle. You can then drive payment-free or sell/trade the car to recoup some value.
Costs: Lower monthly payments (average around $619) and typically requires less money upfront.
Ownership: You are essentially renting the car and paying for its depreciation over the lease term (usually 36 months). At the end, you turn it in or buy it.

Restrictions: Annual mileage caps (usually 10,000 to 15,000 miles) apply. Exceeding limits results in overage penalties (often $0.15 to $0.30 per mile), and you can be charged for excess wear and tear.
Ideal for: Commuters who drive short distances, those who want continuous manufacturer warranty coverage, or business owners who can utilize tax write-offs.

text_blocks.snippet: For a deep-dive analysis on the true total cost of ownership between leasing and buying:

extracted.summary:
**Buying** is best if you want to eventually own the car and drive unlimited miles. While monthly payments are higher, it’s the most cost-effective long-term option once the loan is paid off. **Leasing** is ideal if you want lower monthly payments, lower upfront costs, and the ability to drive a new car with a warranty every few years.[](https://www.consumerreports.org/cars/buying-a-car/leasing-vs-buying-a-new-car-a9135602164/) [[1]](https://www.consumerreports.org/cars/buying-a-car/leasing-vs-buying-a-new-car-a9135602164/)[[2]](https://www.abbank.com/should-you-lease-or-buy-your-next-car-)[[3]](https://www.texasbaycu.org/articles/leasing-vs-buying-whats-the-best-choice-for-you)[[4]](https://kunesstoughton.com/leasing-vs-buying-a-car)

Review the key differences below to determine which option aligns with your driving habits and budget:

Buying

- **Costs:** Higher monthly loan payments (average around $770) and typically requires a 10% to 20% down payment.
- **Ownership:** Once the loan is fully paid off, you own the vehicle. You can then drive payment-free or sell/trade the car to recoup some value.
- **Restrictions:** You have no mileage limits and can customize or sell the car whenever you choose.
- **Long-term:** The cheapest way to drive is to buy a car and keep it for many years after the loan is paid off.[](https://www.consumerreports.org/cars/buying-a-car/leasing-vs-buying-a-new-car-a9135602164/) [[1]](https://www.consumerreports.org/cars/buying-a-car/leasing-vs-buying-a-new-car-a9135602164/)[[2]](https://www.nerdwallet.com/auto-loans/learn/7-lease-vs-buy-questions-right)[[3]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VWmQFz3Vres&t=184)[[4]](https://www.texasbaycu.org/articles/leasing-vs-buying-whats-the-best-choice-for-you)[[5]](https://www.consumerfinance.gov/ask-cfpb/what-should-i-know-about-leasing-versus-buying-a-car-en-815/)[[6]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/y3QCnOvHpwE)[[7]](https://www.navyfederal.org/makingcents/auto/buying-vs-leasing-a-car.html)

Leasing

- **Costs:** Lower monthly payments (average around $619) and typically requires less money upfront.[](https://www.nerdwallet.com/auto-loans/learn/7-lease-vs-buy-questions-right) [[1]](https://www.nerdwallet.com/auto-loans/learn/7-lease-vs-buy-questions-right)[[2]](https://kunesstoughton.com/leasing-vs-buying-a-car)
- **Ownership:** You are essentially renting the car and paying for its depreciation over the lease term (usually 36 months). At the end, you turn it in or buy it.[](https://www.abbank.com/should-you-lease-or-buy-your-next-car-) [[1]](https://www.abbank.com/should-you-lease-or-buy-your-next-car-)[[2]](https://cars.usnews.com/cars-trucks/advice/buying-vs-leasing)
- **Restrictions:** Annual mileage caps (usually 10,000 to 15,000 miles) apply. Exceeding limits results in overage penalties (often $0.15 to $0.30 per mile), and you can be charged for excess wear and tear.[](https://www.consumerreports.org/cars/buying-a-car/leasing-vs-buying-a-new-car-a9135602164/) [[1]](https://www.consumerreports.org/cars/buying-a-car/leasing-vs-buying-a-new-car-a9135602164/)[[2]](https://www.nerdwallet.com/auto-loans/learn/7-lease-vs-buy-questions-right)
- **Ideal for:** Commuters who drive short distances, those who want continuous manufacturer warranty coverage, or business owners who can utilize tax write-offs.[](https://www.investopedia.com/articles/personal-finance/012715/when-leasing-car-better-buying.asp) [[1]](https://www.investopedia.com/articles/personal-finance/012715/when-leasing-car-better-buying.asp)[[2]](https://kunesstoughton.com/leasing-vs-buying-a-car)

- **Costs:** Higher monthly loan payments (average around $770) and typically requires a 10% to 20% down payment.
- **Ownership:** Once the loan is fully paid off, you own the vehicle. You can then drive payment-free or sell/trade the car to recoup some value.

- **Restrictions:** Annual mileage caps (usually 10,000 to 15,000 miles) apply. Exceeding limits results in overage penalties (often $0.15 to $0.30 per mile), and you can be charged for excess wear and tear.[](https://www.consumerreports.org/cars/buying-a-car/leasing-vs-buying-a-new-car-a9135602164/) [[1]](https://www.consumerreports.org/cars/buying-a-car/leasing-vs-buying-a-new-car-a9135602164/)[[2]](https://www.nerdwallet.com/auto-loans/learn/7-lease-vs-buy-questions-right)
